global.title: ACT Encryption and Sharing

coreIdeas.contextPhrase: Wallet-derived AES-256-GCM key — the root of the encryption tree; everything else inherits from this decision.
App-layer encryption responsibility — adapter encrypts before uploading, rather than relying on Swarm features.
Encryption lives at /bytes; feeds stay clean references — the layering invariant.
Concrete encryption checkpoint inside the push cycle.
ACT primitives: per-grantee DH key + encrypted access key in a manifest — the sharing machinery.
Cross-user sharing with ECDH-derived session keys — avoids touching the wallet key.
Parallel formulation: ACT + ECDH per-session key for cross-user share.
ACT replaces legacy deriveShareKey — documents the security improvement.
Adapter-level statement: AES hierarchical feeds for self, ACT for sharing — the clean separation.
Cross-user share bundle = public profile + share manifest + ACT-protected doc.
Phase 2b raw-file sharing via ACT-encrypted /bzz, 200MB cap — recent feature built on this stack.
End-to-end chat-document-share flow that bundles reactor state + unflushed outbox through ACT.

global.description: How Connect encrypts data at the Swarm /bytes layer with AES-256-GCM and uses ACT+ECDH for cross-user sharing without leaking the wallet key.
global.orientation: Connect's privacy story has two layers. At rest, every payload uploaded to /bytes is AES-256-GCM encrypted with a deterministic key derived from a one-time wallet signature so any device with the wallet can recover state. For sharing across users, Connect uses Swarm's ACT with ECDH-derived per-session keys and per-grantee manifests — replacing the older deriveShareKey approach that leaked through publicly derivable SHA-256. This MOC collects the notes that define the encryption layer, the ACT model, and the concrete flows (chat sharing, raw-file sharing, session keys) that depend on them.
